## Binary Search Tree Insertion — Time Complexity

**Average case:** O(log n) — each comparison eliminates roughly half the remaining tree.

**Worst case:** O(n) — occurs when insertions arrive in sorted order, degenerating the tree into a linked list.

### BST Insert Pseudocode

```
function BST_Insert(root, key):
    if root is NULL:
        return new Node(key)
    
    if key < root.key:
        root.left = BST_Insert(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key:
        root.right = BST_Insert(root.right, key)
    
    return root
```

---

## Red-Black Tree: How It Maintains Balance

A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST that enforces **5 invariants**:

| # | Invariant |
|---|-----------|
| 1 | Every node is either **red** or **black** |
| 2 | The **root** is always black |
| 3 | Every **NULL leaf** (sentinel) is black |
| 4 | A **red node** cannot have a red child (no two consecutive reds) |
| 5 | Every path from a node to its descendant NULLs has the **same black-height** |

These guarantee the longest path is at most **2× the shortest path**, bounding height to **O(2 log(n+1))** → insertion is **O(log n) worst case**.

### RB-Tree Insert Pseudocode

```
function RB_Insert(tree, key):
    node = BST_Insert(tree.root, key)   // Standard BST insert
    node.color = RED                     // New nodes always start red
    RB_Insert_Fixup(tree, node)         // Restore invariants

function RB_Insert_Fixup(tree, z):
    while z.parent.color == RED:
        if z.parent == z.grandparent.left:
            uncle = z.grandparent.right

            // --- Case 1: Uncle is RED → recolor ---
            if uncle.color == RED:
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                uncle.color = BLACK
                z.grandparent.color = RED
                z = z.grandparent          // Move problem up

            else:
                // --- Case 2: z is right child → left rotate to Case 3 ---
                if z == z.parent.right:
                    z = z.parent
                    Left_Rotate(tree, z)

                // --- Case 3: z is left child → recolor + right rotate ---
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                z.grandparent.color = RED
                Right_Rotate(tree, z.grandparent)

        else:
            // Mirror: swap left/right in all of the above
            ...

    tree.root.color = BLACK              // Enforce invariant 2
```

### The Three Fix-Up Cases (visually)

```
Case 1: Recolor          Case 2: Rotate to Case 3    Case 3: Rotate + Recolor

    G(B)                      G(B)                        G(B) → P(B)
   / \                       / \                         /        / \
 P(R) U(R)  →  P(B) U(B)  P(R)  U(B)  →  Z(R)  U(B)  Z(R)    G(R)
 /              /             \            /
Z(R)          Z(R)           Z(R)        P(R)
```

### Rotation Pseudocode

```
function Left_Rotate(tree, x):
    y = x.right
    x.right = y.left          // Adopt y's left subtree
    if y.left ≠ NULL:
        y.left.parent = x
    y.parent = x.parent       // y takes x's position
    if x.parent == NULL:
        tree.root = y
    else if x == x.parent.left:
        x.parent.left = y
    else:
        x.parent.right = y
    y.left = x                // x becomes y's left child
    x.parent = y
```

Right rotation is the mirror image.

---

## Complexity Summary

| Operation | BST (worst) | BST (avg) | Red-Black Tree (worst) |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|------------------------|
| Insert    | O(n)        | O(log n)  | **O(log n)**           |
| Search    | O(n)        | O(log n)  | **O(log n)**           |
| Delete    | O(n)        | O(log n)  | **O(log n)**           |
| Rotations on insert | — | —       | **≤ 2**                |

The key insight: new nodes are inserted as **red** (to avoid violating invariant 5), and then at most **O(log n) recolorings** and **≤ 2 rotations** restore all invariants, keeping the tree balanced.
